meta.title: Performance & Load Testing
meta.description: Load testing with k6, JMeter, Locust, Lighthouse audits, and performance profiling

items.text: Does the load test simulate realistic traffic patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Load tests should model real user behavior: ramp-up time, think time between requests, mix of endpoints, and realistic data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Blasting 10,000 RPS at one endpoint doesn't simulate reality. Real traffic has varied patterns, concurrent users, and mixed operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for ramp-up stages, think time (sleep between requests), and a mix of read/write operations matching production ratios.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test sends 1000 RPS to /api/users instantly. Real traffic ramps up gradually. Add stages: 0->100->500->1000 over 10 minutes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Model real traffic: ramp-up, think time, endpoint mix. Don't just blast one endpoint.

items.text: Are pass/fail thresholds defined (p95 response time, error rate)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Define explicit pass/fail criteria: p95 response time < 500ms, error rate < 1%, throughput > 100 RPS.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without thresholds, load tests produce data but never fail. A 5-second p95 response time passes because nobody defined 'too slow'.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for threshold definitions in load test configuration. Verify thresholds align with SLOs.
baseContent.exampleComment: This k6 test has no thresholds. Add: thresholds: { http_req_duration: ['p(95)<500'], http_req_failed: ['rate<0.01'] }.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define thresholds for every load test. Align with SLOs: p95 latency, error rate, throughput.

items.text: Are stress tests run to find the breaking point?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Beyond normal load tests, run stress tests that increase load until the system breaks. Find the ceiling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Knowing the breaking point helps capacity planning. You need to know if the system handles 2x or 10x current traffic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for stress test scenarios that ramp beyond expected load. Verify the breaking point is documented.
baseContent.exampleComment: Load tests only test normal traffic (100 RPS). Add a stress test that ramps to 1000 RPS to find the breaking point.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run stress tests to find breaking points. Know your system's ceiling for capacity planning.

items.text: Are soak tests run for memory leaks and degradation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run extended load tests (1-8 hours) at normal traffic to detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and gradual degradation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Memory leaks and resource exhaustion don't show in 5-minute tests. They appear after hours of steady load.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for long-duration test scenarios. Verify memory usage, connection counts, and response times are monitored over time.
baseContent.exampleComment: All load tests run for 5 minutes. Add a 2-hour soak test at normal load to detect memory leaks and connection pool issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run soak tests for hours to find memory leaks and resource exhaustion.

items.text: Are tests run against production-like data volumes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Load tests should run against databases with production-like data volumes. 100 rows vs 10M rows produces very different results.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A query that's fast on 100 rows can be catastrophically slow on 10M rows. Missing indexes only matter at scale.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the test database data volume. Compare to production. Verify indexes and query plans match production.
baseContent.exampleComment: Load tests run against a database with 100 users. Production has 5M users. Seed the test database with realistic volume.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test against production-like data volumes. Performance at 100 rows doesn't predict performance at 10M.

items.text: Are Core Web Vitals measured and within thresholds (LCP, FID, CLS)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Measure Largest Contentful Paint (<2.5s), First Input Delay (<100ms), and Cumulative Layout Shift (<0.1) for key pages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Core Web Vitals affect SEO ranking and user experience. Google uses them as ranking signals.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run Lighthouse CI on key pages. Check that LCP, FID/INP, and CLS meet 'good' thresholds.
baseContent.exampleComment: The homepage LCP is 4.2s (poor). Optimize the hero image: use next/image, add priority loading, serve WebP format.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Measure Core Web Vitals in CI. LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1.

items.text: Is bundle size monitored with size budgets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set size budgets for JS bundles. Track bundle size over time. Alert when bundles exceed thresholds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bundle size creeps up over time. Without monitoring, a 200KB bundle becomes 2MB and nobody notices until users complain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for bundlesize, size-limit, or webpack performance hints configuration. Verify size budgets are set.
baseContent.exampleComment: No bundle size monitoring. Add size-limit with a 250KB budget for the main bundle. CI will fail if it's exceeded.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set bundle size budgets. Monitor and alert on size increases in CI.

items.text: Is Lighthouse CI integrated into the build pipeline?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run Lighthouse CI on every PR to catch performance regressions. Set minimum scores for performance, accessibility, and best practices.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Performance regressions are easy to introduce and hard to notice. Automated Lighthouse checks catch them before merge.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration for Lighthouse CI steps. Verify minimum score thresholds are configured.
baseContent.exampleComment: No Lighthouse CI configured. Add lighthouse-ci with minimum scores: performance 90, accessibility 95, best-practices 90.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run Lighthouse CI on every PR. Set minimum score thresholds.

items.text: Are images optimized (format, size, lazy loading)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use modern formats (WebP, AVIF), appropriate sizes, and lazy loading for below-fold images.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unoptimized images are the #1 cause of slow page loads. A single 5MB PNG can make the page unusable on mobile.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for unoptimized images: large file sizes, PNG where WebP works, missing lazy loading, missing srcset for responsive.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 3MB PNG hero image loads on every page. Convert to WebP (300KB), add srcset for responsive sizes, lazy-load if below fold.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Optimize images: modern formats, responsive sizes, lazy loading. Images are usually the biggest payload.

items.text: Are unnecessary re-renders detected and prevented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Profile component renders. Avoid unnecessary re-renders from unstable references, missing memoization, or context overuse.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary re-renders cause jank, slow interactions, and battery drain on mobile. A list of 1000 items re-rendering on every keystroke freezes the UI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Use React DevTools Profiler or similar to check render counts. Verify large lists use virtualization.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component re-renders 50 times on a single keystroke because the parent passes a new object reference every render.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Profile render performance. Use memoization, virtualization, and stable references.

items.text: Are database queries profiled for N+1 and slow queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Profile database queries for N+1 patterns, missing indexes, full table scans, and excessive query counts per request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the most common backend performance issue. Loading 100 items with N+1 sends 101 queries instead of 2.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check query counts per API request. Look for loops that execute queries. Verify eager loading for related data.
baseContent.exampleComment: Loading the order list executes 1 query for orders + 1 per order for items (N+1). Use eager loading or a JOIN.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Profile queries. Fix N+1 with eager loading. Add indexes for slow queries.

items.text: Are frequently accessed endpoints cached appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cache expensive computations and frequently read data. Use appropriate cache headers for HTTP caching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hitting the database for the same unchanged data on every request wastes resources and adds latency.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for caching on read-heavy endpoints. Verify cache invalidation on writes. Check HTTP cache headers.
baseContent.exampleComment: The product catalog endpoint hits the database on every request. Cache it with a 5-minute TTL — products rarely change.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cache read-heavy endpoints. Use appropriate TTLs and invalidation strategies.

items.text: Are connection pool sizes appropriate for expected load?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database and HTTP connection pool sizes should match expected concurrency. Too small causes queuing, too large wastes resources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pool exhaustion under load causes requests to queue and timeout. Oversized pools waste database connections.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check pool size configuration. Verify it aligns with expected concurrent requests and database connection limits.
baseContent.exampleComment: Default pool size of 5 connections with 100 concurrent requests causes 95 to queue. Increase to 20-30 based on load testing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Size connection pools based on load testing. Monitor pool usage in production.

items.text: Are slow operations processed asynchronously (queues, background jobs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations that take >500ms (email sending, report generation, image processing) should be queued for background processing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Synchronous slow operations block the request thread, reducing throughput and causing timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for synchronous operations that are slow: email, file processing, external API calls. These should be async.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint generates a PDF synchronously, taking 5 seconds. Queue the generation and return 202 Accepted with a status URL.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Queue slow operations. Return 202 Accepted with a status endpoint for long-running tasks.

items.text: Are memory usage patterns tested for leaks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Profile memory usage during load tests. Check for growing memory that isn't released (event listeners, caches, closures).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Memory leaks cause OOM crashes in production. They only manifest under sustained load over time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Monitor memory usage during soak tests. Check for steadily increasing memory that doesn't plateau.
baseContent.exampleComment: Memory grows from 200MB to 2GB over 2 hours of steady load. Profile for leaked event listeners or unbounded caches.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor memory during soak tests. Steadily growing memory indicates a leak.

items.text: Are performance baselines established and tracked over time?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Record performance baselines (p50, p95, p99 response times) and track them across releases to detect regressions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without baselines, you can't detect gradual degradation. A 10% regression per release compounds to 50% over 5 releases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for performance tracking dashboards. Verify baselines are recorded and compared across releases.
baseContent.exampleComment: No performance baselines recorded. Run benchmark tests and record p50/p95/p99 for key endpoints as the baseline.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Establish baselines. Track p50/p95/p99 across releases to catch regressions.

items.text: Are performance alerts configured for SLO violations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set up alerts when performance metrics exceed SLO thresholds: latency spikes, error rate increases, throughput drops.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without alerts, performance issues go unnoticed until users complain. Proactive alerting catches issues early.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for performance alert rules in monitoring configuration. Verify they're based on SLOs, not arbitrary thresholds.
baseContent.exampleComment: No performance alerts configured. Add alerts for: p95 latency > 500ms, error rate > 1%, throughput < 80% of baseline.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Alert on SLO violations. Use p95 latency and error rate as primary indicators.

items.text: Are profiling tools used to identify bottlenecks (not guessing)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use profilers (flame graphs, CPU profiles, memory snapshots) to identify actual bottlenecks before optimizing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Optimizing without profiling is guessing. The actual bottleneck is rarely where you think it is.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if performance improvements are backed by profiling data. Verify the optimization addresses the actual bottleneck.
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR optimizes string concatenation but the profile shows 90% of time is in database queries. Profile first, optimize second.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Profile before optimizing. Use flame graphs and CPU profiles to find real bottlenecks.

items.text: Are performance regression tests integrated into CI?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Run micro-benchmarks or targeted load tests in CI to catch performance regressions before they reach production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Performance regressions caught in CI are cheap to fix. Found in production, they require emergency hotfixes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI for performance test stages. Verify they compare against baselines and fail on significant regressions.
baseContent.exampleComment: No performance tests in CI. Add benchmark tests for critical code paths that fail if performance degrades >10%.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run performance tests in CI. Fail the build on significant regressions.

items.text: Is Real User Monitoring (RUM) collecting actual user performance data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Collect performance metrics from real users' browsers/devices. Synthetic tests don't capture real-world conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Lab tests run on fast machines with good connections. RUM shows actual performance on users' slow phones and spotty WiFi.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for RUM integration (Web Vitals, Datadog RUM, New Relic Browser). Verify data is segmented by device and connection.
baseContent.exampleComment: Only synthetic Lighthouse tests are used. Add RUM to capture real user metrics and compare with lab results.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Collect real user metrics alongside synthetic tests. Real-world performance differs from lab results.

items.text: Is the application tested with multiple instances (horizontal scaling)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test the application running on multiple instances behind a load balancer. Verify sessions, state, and file uploads work correctly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code that stores state in memory (sessions, file uploads, caches) breaks when running on multiple instances.
baseContent.howToVerify: Deploy multiple instances and run load tests. Verify sticky sessions aren't required. Check for in-memory state.
baseContent.exampleComment: File uploads are stored in local temp directories. With 2 instances, the download request may hit the other instance. Use S3.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test with multiple instances. Eliminate local state: use shared storage and external session stores.

items.text: Are database performance tests run with expected growth projections?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test database performance with data volumes expected at 6 months and 1 year of growth. Plan for data growth.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Queries fast on current data may be catastrophically slow at 10x volume. Planning prevents emergency migrations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if load tests account for data growth. Verify indexes and query plans work at projected volumes.
baseContent.exampleComment: Database has 1M rows now, growing 100K/month. Test performance at 3M and 5M rows to catch future issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test at projected data volumes. Plan for growth before it becomes an emergency.

items.text: Does the system degrade gracefully under extreme load?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Under extreme load, the system should slow down gracefully, not crash. Implement circuit breakers, rate limiting, and load shedding.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A system that crashes under load causes total outage. Graceful degradation serves some users while protecting the system.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run load tests beyond capacity. Verify the system returns errors gracefully, doesn't crash, and recovers automatically.
baseContent.exampleComment: At 5x normal load, the application OOMs and crashes. Implement rate limiting and circuit breakers to shed excess load.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Systems should degrade gracefully, not crash. Use circuit breakers and rate limiting.
